title: Lap 1 — Getting Started with Dynatrace
description: Your first lap. Learn what observability means, where to find help, and how Dynatrace helps with Observability.
imagine: Your team has just migrated to Dynatrace. Before the race starts, every good crew member needs to know where the tools are.
yourGoal: Complete 5 questions to earn your paddock pass. No prior Dynatrace experience needed — this is your starting grid.
tools: Dynatrace Platform · Dynatrace Docs (docs.dynatrace.com) · Dynatrace Support (support.dynatrace.com)
note: These questions are designed to help you build confidence and familiarity with Dynatrace as you move away from legacy monitoring tools.

story.introduction: Teams moving from legacy tools often ask 'Where do I go for help?' or 'How is Dynatrace different from what we used before?' This scenario answers both.
story.context: Dynatrace goes beyond static monitoring — it maps every dependency in your environment, uses AI to find root causes automatically, and surfaces answers before your customers notice a problem.

questions.title: What does Observability mean?
questions.content: You have heard the word 'observability' used a lot since Dynatrace arrived. Which of the following best describes what it means?

options.text: Watching CPU, memory, and disk on servers — in a Dashboard
options.explanation: That is traditional infrastructure monitoring. Observability is a broader concept covering the entire system.

options.text: The ability to understand the internal state of a system from the data it produces — what happened, why, and where
options.explanation: Correct! Observability means you can ask any question about your system and get an answer from the data it emits.
More dashboards does not equal more observability. You need the right signals and the ability to ask new questions.

options.text: Watching logs scroll by in real time
options.explanation: Logs are one input to observability, but on their own they do not give you the full picture.

questions.explanation: Observability is the ability to ask any question about your system and get an answer from the data it produces — not just pre-defined metrics.
hints: Think beyond dashboards — observability is about understanding what happened and why, not just watching values change.

questions.title: The Three Pillars
questions.content: Observability is built on three types of telemetry data. Which combination makes up the three pillars?

options.explanation: These are outputs — presentations of data — not the underlying telemetry signals.
These are types of metrics. Metrics are just one of the three pillars.
Correct! Metrics answer 'how much?', logs answer 'what exactly happened?', and traces answer 'how did the request travel across services?' with security providing insights if your code is vulnerable.
These are the entities you observe, not the types of data you collect to observe them.

questions.explanation: Metrics show trends, logs capture events in detail, and traces follow a request end-to-end across services. Dynatrace collects all three automatically.
hints: Think about the different questions your system data can answer: how much, what happened, and how requests flow through the system.

questions.title: Dynatrace is only for infrastructure
questions.content: Dynatrace only monitors infrastructure (servers and network) — not the applications running on top of them.
questions.explanation: False. Dynatrace provides full-stack observability — from the user's browser click, through every microservice and database call, all the way down to the host and container.
hints: Think about whether Dynatrace stops at servers, or follows a user request through applications and services.

questions.title: Getting help with Dynatrace
questions.content: You are stuck while using Dynatrace and want to get help or guidance. Which of the following are valid ways to get support or self‑help for Dynatrace?

options.text: Go to help.dynatrace.com to read official Dynatrace documentation
options.explanation: Correct resource, but this is only one of several valid help options.

options.text: Go to support.dynatrace.com and create a support case
options.explanation: Correct for product issues and bugs, but not the only way to get help.

options.text: Use support.dynatrace.com to open a chat with Dynatrace Support
options.explanation: Chat is available for many accounts, but it is not the only help channel.
Correct. Dynatrace offers official documentation, support cases, and live support options depending on your need.

questions.explanation: Dynatrace supports a self‑help‑first model. Official documentation (help.dynatrace.com) is ideal for learning and how‑to guidance, while support.dynatrace.com is used to raise cases or engage with support for product issues.
hints: Consider all the official ways Dynatrace helps users learn, troubleshoot, and get support.

questions.title: Davis AI finds root cause automatically
questions.content: Davis AI automatically analyses your environment, correlates events across your full stack, and identifies the root cause of problems — without you needing to manually dig through dashboards and logs.
questions.explanation: True. Davis AI is always on, continuously learning your baseline. The moment it detects an anomaly it runs causal analysis and surfaces the root cause — often before your customers notice.
hints: Think about whether Dynatrace requires manual correlation, or if AI assists by connecting signals automatically.

learningObjectives: Understand what observability means and how it differs from traditional monitoring
Name the three pillars of observability
Locate official Dynatrace documentation and support channels
Understand how Davis AI accelerates root-cause analysis
